explanation: Empirical therapy is started before the causative organism is identified, using broad-spectrum cover, which is then de-escalated once cultures return.

statements.text: it is used when the cause of infection is exactly known
statements.explanation: Empirical therapy is by definition started before the organism is known; targeted therapy is used once it is identified.

statements.text: macrolides should be avoided
statements.explanation: There is no blanket rule against macrolides; choice depends on the likely pathogen and local guidance.

statements.text: broad spectrum agents should be used initially
statements.explanation: Initial empirical cover is deliberately broad to cover likely pathogens until cultures guide narrowing.

statements.text: it should always use a single agent
statements.explanation: Combination therapy is often used empirically (e.g. severe sepsis) depending on the suspected source.

statements.text: prolonged administration of broad-spectrum coverage should be avoided
statements.explanation: Therapy should be de-escalated promptly to limit resistance and toxicity once culture results are available.

stem: Reasons for no response to train-of-four (TOF) stimulation, 90 min after the last dose of muscle relaxant include
explanation: Persistent absent TOF reflects ongoing neuromuscular block — from drug sensitivity or impaired metabolism — not from anaesthetic depth or opioids, which do not act at the neuromuscular junction.

statements.text: myasthenia gravis
statements.explanation: Myasthenic patients are markedly sensitive to non-depolarising blockers, producing prolonged block.

statements.text: excessive depth of anaesthesia
statements.explanation: Anaesthetic depth does not abolish the TOF response; the neuromuscular junction is unaffected.

statements.text: a genetic cholinesterase abnormality
statements.explanation: Atypical plasma cholinesterase markedly prolongs suxamethonium/mivacurium block.

statements.text: phenytoin therapy for epilepsy
statements.explanation: Chronic phenytoin induces resistance to non-depolarising blockers (faster recovery), not prolonged block.

statements.text: high dose remifentanil infusion
statements.explanation: Opioids have no action at the neuromuscular junction and do not abolish TOF.

stem: Use of ketamine in paediatric anaesthesia
explanation: Ketamine preserves spontaneous ventilation and a relatively patent airway and is useful for IM induction with poor venous access, but it does not protect against aspiration or reliably prevent laryngospasm.

statements.text: is recommended as the sole anaesthetic for children with a full stomach
statements.explanation: Ketamine does not protect the airway against aspiration; a full stomach still mandates airway protection.

statements.text: by the intramuscular route (up to 10 mg/kg) permits line insertion in children with poor venous access
statements.explanation: IM ketamine is a recognised technique to achieve dissociation and allow cannulation.

statements.text: is the induction agent of choice for children with an active upper respiratory tract infection
statements.explanation: Elective anaesthesia is generally deferred with an active URTI; ketamine is not an indication to proceed.

statements.text: avoids laryngospasm
statements.explanation: Laryngospasm can still occur; ketamine also increases secretions, which may aggravate it.

statements.text: is associated with maintenance of spontaneous respiration and a patent airway during induction
statements.explanation: Relative preservation of ventilation and airway tone is a characteristic feature of ketamine.

explanation: Eclamptic seizures can occur ante-, intra- or postpartum and even with normal blood pressure; magnesium sulfate is first-line and intracranial haemorrhage is a recognised complication of severe hypertension.

statements.text: seizures can occur after delivery
statements.explanation: Postpartum eclampsia is well recognised, sometimes days after delivery.

statements.text: seizures can occur in parturients with normal blood pressure
statements.explanation: Atypical/normotensive eclampsia is described; normal BP does not exclude it.

statements.text: an intravenous infusion of magnesium is the agent of choice to treat seizures
statements.explanation: Magnesium sulfate is the first-line drug for treating and preventing eclamptic seizures.

statements.text: intracranial hemorrhage is a complication of severe hypertension
statements.explanation: Intracerebral haemorrhage is a major cause of death in severe pre-eclampsia/eclampsia.

statements.text: a long period of pre-eclampsia precedes symptoms
statements.explanation: Eclampsia can occur abruptly without a prolonged preceding pre-eclamptic phase.

stem: Comatose patients after a motor vehicle accident
explanation: Airway protection with cervical-spine precautions is the priority; routine high-dose barbiturates are not indicated and a MAP target of 60 mmHg is too low when traumatic brain injury is likely.

statements.text: must be protected against aspiration
statements.explanation: A reduced conscious level mandates airway protection against aspiration.

statements.text: should be intubated using high-dose rocuronium for rapid onset of neuromuscular blockade
statements.explanation: Rapid-sequence induction is appropriate, but routine 'high-dose rocuronium' is not the standard prescription as stated.

statements.text: should be treated with high-dose barbiturates
statements.explanation: Barbiturate coma is a last-line measure for refractory raised ICP, not routine management.

statements.text: require rapid fluid resuscitation to a target mean arterial pressure of 60 mmHg
statements.explanation: With likely traumatic brain injury a higher MAP is needed to maintain cerebral perfusion; 60 mmHg is too low.

statements.text: should be intubated after a rigid cervical spine collar has been placed
statements.explanation: Cervical-spine protection is required; manual in-line stabilisation is used during laryngoscopy.

stem: An oliguric patient after kidney transplantation has a serum potassium level of 7.1 mmol/L
explanation: Severe hyperkalaemia is managed with calcium for myocardial membrane stabilisation plus insulin/dextrose to shift potassium intracellularly; haemodialysis (not CVVH) is the fastest route of removal, and bicarbonate is not first-line.

statements.text: soluble insulin can be given to move K⁺ into cells
statements.explanation: Insulin (with dextrose) is a standard measure to shift potassium intracellularly.

statements.text: in the absence of cardiac arrhythmia, intravenous administration of furosemide is appropriate
statements.explanation: If any renal function remains, a loop diuretic promotes kaliuresis as an adjunct.

statements.text: continuous venovenous haemofiltration is the method of choice to remove K⁺ rapidly from the body
statements.explanation: Intermittent haemodialysis removes potassium far faster than CVVH for life-threatening hyperkalaemia.

statements.text: intravenous infusion of sodium bicarbonate is the first choice therapy
statements.explanation: Bicarbonate is adjunctive; calcium (cardioprotection) and insulin/dextrose are higher priority.

statements.text: intravenous Ca²⁺ should be administered
statements.explanation: At K⁺ 7.1 mmol/L IV calcium is given to stabilise the myocardium against arrhythmia.
